text: The surgeon inspects the hernia sac to confirm that its contents are omentum, ensuring there are no bowel loops present.
The surgeon uses an energy device to sharply dissect thick fibrous strands within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses sharp cold scissors to carefully dissect tissue, avoiding monopolar energy due to the proximity of the bowel.
The surgeon confirms that the hernia contents are omentum without any bubble present, allowing the use of monopolar dissection to proceed safely.
The surgeon identifies and dissects thick fibrous strands, revealing underlying fat that can be reduced with gentle manipulation.
The surgeon gently manipulates the fat during the hernia reduction, ensuring not to tug too firmly to prevent damage to the omentum and avoid bleeding.
The surgeon proceeds slowly with step-by-step dissection, applying counter pressure from outside to aid in the careful separation of tissues.
The surgeon reduces one portion of the omentum, then proceeds to reduce the remaining omentum in a clockwise manner, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon identifies fibrous strands located towards the end of the omentum structure, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies and divides the remaining thick fibrous strands using a monopolar cautery, allowing the small residual content to be pulled and reduced.
The surgeon has successfully reduced the entire hernia content, revealing the underlying anatomical structures without any visible tension or damage.
The surgeon carefully inspects the hernia sac to ensure no remnant omentum is left behind, preventing potential gangrenous complications.
The surgeon identifies a small defect through the primary defect after measuring the area, revealing the defect's location and size within the surgical field.
The surgeon makes a small incision in the preparatorial layer approximately seven centimeters above the apical portion of the hernia defect, raising a flap for further surgical access.
The surgeon begins dissection to demonstrate a surgical plane suitable for incisional, primary umbilical, or paraumbilical hernia repair, focusing on the anatomical layers involved.

The surgeon opens the pre-peritoneal plane, which is not the plane described by Stopa, but is suitable for hernia surgery. The dissection reveals a small buttonhole on the posterior rectus sheath as the surgeon goes deeper.
The surgeon divides the rectus sheath deeper, approaching the muscle layer, demonstrating the proper Stopa technique for hernia repair.
The surgeon enters the retrorectus plane, which is the typical Stoppa plane, preparing to place the mesh within this anatomical space.
The surgeon exposes the transversalis fascia and a small portion of the posterior rectus sheath, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum along with the sheath surrounding the hernia defect, revealing fibrous tissue that prevents complete dissection of the sac.
The surgeon enters the stirpass plane just below the hernia defect, dissecting the preperitoneal space to prepare for mesh placement.
A pad of fat and the transversalis fascia are visible, along with the surgical flap, positioned such that the muscle lies flat beneath them.
The border of the rectus muscle becomes visible in the surgical field, clearly delineating its position.
The surgeon divides small fibrous fatty structures, quickly opening up the surgical plane in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric artery positioned just above the surgical instrument, having entered the lateral portion beyond the rectus muscle.
The surgeon displays the fully dissected area, highlighting the minimal rectal diastasis located just below the hernia defect.

The surgeon creates a small stab incision on the lower aspect of the hernia defect and takes a bite on one edge of the defect. The defect measures approximately 3 by 2.5 centimeters, and a primary closure is performed.
The surgeon takes suturing bites on either side of the hernia defect edge, with external manipulation aiding in the process to ensure precise closure of the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon takes bites on both sides of the hernia defect, ensuring a good thickness of the edge is captured. The thread is elongated progressively to maintain adequate length for suturing.
The surgeon focuses on suturing the linea alba without involving excessive thickness of the rectus abdominis muscle, aiming to bring the muscles together at the midline.
The surgeon retrieves one limb of the Vicryl suture that was placed on the polypropylene mesh, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon places a 15x15 cm lightweight polypropylene macroporous mesh in the surgical field. Two transfacial sutures are taken in the midline, and the mesh is anchored anteriorly using intracorporeal sutures.

The surgeon anchors the remaining sutures using a 2-0 Vicryl absorbable material after placing two central trans-facial sutures. The sutures are placed in the midline to avoid nerve entrapment and chronic pain, ensuring a secure hold in the midline.
The surgeon takes suturing bites at regular intervals to loosely secure the polypropylene mesh anteriorly, ensuring the mesh remains in place without causing excessive tension or muscle necrosis.

The surgeon anchors the upper left corner of the polypropylene mesh to the muscle using intracorporeal suturing. The mesh is neatly positioned on the anterior abdominal wall, completing the incisional hernia repair.
The surgeon sutures the defect created by the dissection around the hernia defect in the flap using Vicryl sutures.
The surgeon completes suturing of the flap and raises it to check for any buttonhole defects, finding none. The right side edge of the flap is then closed using a running suture technique.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing a wide area covered by the mesh on the abdominal wall. The mesh is securely in place, restoring the abdominal wall's physiology. The flap is intact without any buttonhole defects, and sutures are taken at regular intervals.
